text: Total laparoscopic first IMV approach for splenic flexural tumor. Forty-nine-year-old female presents with anemia and blood stall. Colonoscopy shows a splenic flexural tumor. Biopsy reported as mucinous adenocarcinoma. There is no evidence of metastasis.
The patient is positioned supine on the operating table with the right arm tucked alongside the body.

The surgeon inserts a 10mm trocar above the umbilicus for a 30-degree laparoscopic camera, along with a 10mm port in the right lower quadrant, a 5mm trocar in the right upper quadrant for working ports, and a 5mm trocar in the left quadrant for assistance.

The surgeon begins the operation by visualizing the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and creating a window through the peritoneum. Using gentle blunt dissection, the surgeon separates Gerota's fascia and Toldt's fascia through the embryological planes. Once the dissection is complete, the IMV is clipped and ligated safely below the pancreas.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery pedicle and the left colic artery for the splenic flexural cancer. The left colic artery is clipped and ligated, while the sigmoid artery is preserved to ensure adequate blood supply for the anastomosis between the transverse and sigmoid colon.
The surgeon continues the dissection through Gerota's and Toldt's fascia, progressing until the spleen and lateral abdominal wall are identified.

The surgeon places a gauze on the retroperitoneum after visualizing the spleen above the pancreas. The gastrocolic ligament is opened, and the greater omentum of the splenic flexure is ligated. Bipolar devices are used to ligate the splenocolic ligaments. The lateral toes fascia is opened, and the left colon is mobilized.
The surgeon fully mobilizes the splenic flexure, ligating and dividing the last branch of the splenocolic ligaments.
The surgeon ligates the left branch of the middle colic artery near the proximal surgical margin. A laparoscopic linear stapler is used to transect the transverse colon.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the sigmoid colon, opening the lateral fascia with the aid of a previously placed gauze on the retroperitoneum. During mobilization, the left ureter, gonadal arteries, and iliac vessels are carefully identified. An energy device is used to thin the sigmoid colon, followed by transection using a laparoscopic linear stapler.

The surgeon externalizes the specimen to the left upper quadrant and sutures the transverse and sigmoid colon side to side. Holes are made for the laparoscopic linear stapler, which is used to perform a side-to-side anastomosis. The stapler window is then closed with two-layer continuous sutures.

The specimen is extracted from the camera port using a specimen bag. The pathology report reveals adenocarcinoma with over fifty percent mucinous component, and thirty-three lymph nodes are dissected, showing no evidence of metastasis.
